title: China can do it for free?
text:
D

DanX
Active member

This shows the real power of China’s production capacity​

Overcapacity in China’s Smelters: Chinese Factories Offer Zero-Charge Copper Smelting for Chilean Miners​Over the past few decades, the copper industrial chain has operated under a clear commercial logic: mines sell copper concentrates to smelters and pay a Treatment Charge (TC), allowing smelters to earn steady profits from processing. Today, this long-standing framework is being completely upended.

Data from Mysteel shows domestic spot TC has slumped to -$125 per dry metric ton, while the 2026 long-term contract TC stands at $0 per dry metric ton.

In December 2025, Chinese smelters and Chilean mining firm Antofagasta finalized the full-year 2026 long-term TC for copper concentrates at $0 per ton and 0 cents per pound — marking the first zero processing fee in the history of the copper industry. This figure represents a sharp plunge from the 2025 levels of $21.25 per ton and 2.125 cents per pound.

Conditions in the spot market are even more extreme. The crude smelting TC fell steadily from single-digit levels at the start of 2025, sinking into negative territory below -$40 per ton this year.

Persistent negative TCs have completely overturned smelters’ traditional profit model built on processing fees. Instead of receiving payment for smelting, smelters must pay miners to process their ore.

As copper prices hover above the $10,000-per-ton threshold, midstream players across the industrial chain are suffering a severe profit squeeze. This reflects a fundamental mismatch between global copper mine supply and smelting demand.

Li Chengbin, copper ore analyst at Mysteel, told Jiemian News that global smelting capacity has expanded rapidly at a pace far outstripping mine supply. Between 2023 and 2025, China added 2.65 million metric tons per annum of new copper smelting capacity, while overseas capacity rose by 1.75 million metric tons per annum. An additional 800,000 metric tons per annum of new smelting capacity is projected to come online in 2026.

By contrast, mine output has only increased by roughly 1 million metric tons per annum. Furthermore, as major mining regions including Chile and Peru mature, ore grades at mines continue to decline, operational disruptions have become more frequent, and supply volumes have shrunk. This persistent shortage of copper concentrates has given mines and traders the upper hand in negotiations, leaving smelters with weak bargaining power.

Xu Pengfei explained that the industry does not follow a simple rule of cutting output once losses emerge. Copper smelting relies on continuous production. Constraints such as fulfilling long-term contracts, maintaining equipment stability, sustaining sulfuric acid production lines, preserving cash flow, and meeting local GDP output targets make it difficult for smelters to shut down furnaces on a large scale quickly, even when processing margins shrink to minimal levels.

“To feed newly added capacity or fill raw material shortfalls for existing production lines, smelters are scrambling to secure ore on the spot market regardless of costs. Profit margins have shifted upstream toward mines, giving mining companies their strongest bargaining position in years,” Li Chengbin stated.

Over the past two years, prices of precious metals such as gold and silver, minor metals including antimony and germanium, and sulfuric acid have remained elevated. Li Chengbin noted that robust earnings from these by-products provide a substantial profit buffer for smelters, enabling them to tolerate extremely low or even negative processing fees.

Analysts at UK-based commodities research consultancy CRU pointed out that processing fees accounted for 39% of smelters’ total revenue back in 2018. Last year, however, free metals and by-product revenue (primarily sulfuric acid) became smelters’ top two income streams, making up 50–53% and 25–27% of total revenue respectively.
